section.title: Introduction and Overview
section.p: CMunge is a tool for RISC OS developers that generates the "glue" code (veneers) needed to write RISC OS Relocatable Modules in C or C++. It is designed as a free, enhanced alternative to the Acorn/Castle C Module Header Generator (CMHG).

CMunge reads the same input files as CMHG and, coupled with an assembler, performs the same task. It produces a textual output file suitable for feeding to an assembler, which can then be automatically invoked to produce an object file.

CMunge bridges the gap between the RISC OS kernel and the C development environment. It handles the complexities of the module header structure, SWI dispatching, command-line parsing, and interrupt handling.
A key strength of CMunge is its ability to generate code for multiple ARM architectures, including legacy 26-bit, standard 32-bit, and 64-bit (AArch64) support.

subsection.title: Differences from CMHG
subsection.p: While CMHG produces an AOF output file directly, CMunge produces an assembly source file. CMunge can then invoke an assembler to produce the final object file.

CMunge adds several features not found in original CMHG versions:
Error base handling and error identifier generation.
SWI-specific handlers in the decoding table.
Vector trap handlers.
Parameters for generic veneers (private-word and carry-capable).
Vector handlers capable of claiming with errors.
Simple additive evaluation of integers.
Enhanced C++ module support.
Non-reentrant module support (module-is-not-reentrant).
Command keyword handlers and handler omission (handler and no-handler).
Extended preprocessing that processes included file content (-px).
Support for multiple toolchains (Norcroft, GCC, LCC).
64-bit (AArch64) code generation.

In the generated header, CMunge defines CMUNGE_VERSION to its version, and CMHG_VERSION to 531 for compatibility. Module_VersionString is defined as the full version string in CMunge, whereas CMHG defines it as just the version number.

subsection.title: Preprocessing
subsection.p: CMunge can invoke an external C preprocessor to handle #include, #define, and conditional compilation (#ifdef) within the CMHG description file.

item.p: -p: Standard preprocessing. Included files are used only for macro definitions; their textual content is ignored by CMunge.
-px: Extended preprocessing. CMunge will process the entire flattened output of the preprocessor, including content from included files.

category.title: Norcroft (DDE)
category.p:
The default toolchain for standard RISC OS development.
Preprocessing: cc -E -C++
Assembly: objasm

category.title: GCC
category.p:
Used for cross-compilation or native GCC-based development, and required for 64-bit AArch64 code generation.
Preprocessing: gcc -E -nostdinc -xc
Assembly: gcc -c -xassembler (or custom assembler via CMUNGE_AS_AARCH32 / CMUNGE_AS_AARCH64)
Note: Dependency file generation (-depend) is not supported with the GCC toolchain on non-RISC OS platforms.

category.title: LCC
category.p: LCC support is considered experimental and effectively unusable: LCC does not support generation of relocatable modules, and filenames passed to its cpp must be in Unix form, whereas CMunge uses native RISC OS filenames. Dependency file generation and throwback error reporting are also unsupported with this toolchain. The -tlcc option is retained for completeness only.

subsection.title: Architecture Support
subsection.p: CMunge supports three target architectures, selected by the -26bit, -32bit, and -64bit command-line flags.

category.title: 26-bit (default)
category.p: Generates legacy RISC OS ARM code using the -26bit flag (or by default if no architecture is specified). The 26-bit PC format embeds mode bits in the lower bits of the program counter.

category.title: 32-bit
category.p: Generates code compatible with 32-bit RISC OS using the -32bit flag. The 32-bit veneers are "neutral" — they function identically on both 26-bit and 32-bit environments. This is the recommended mode for modern RISC OS development. For 32-bit builds, the recommended APCS specifier is:
For brevity, since the defaults are suitable, this can be abbreviated to:

category.title: 64-bit (AArch64)
category.p: Generates code for the AArch64 architecture using the -64bit flag. This requires the GCC toolchain (-tgcc). The assembler paths can be overridden using environment variables:

definition-table.definition: Path to the 32-bit ARM assembler for GCC builds. Overrides the default riscos-as.
Path to the 64-bit AArch64 assembler for GCC 64-bit builds. Overrides the default riscos64-as.

subsection.title: Explicit APCS Specification
subsection.p: The ARM Procedure Call Standard (APCS) variant can be specified using the -apcs switch. The general form is -apcs 3/<flags>. CMunge supports a subset of variants:

definition-table.definition: Pass floating point arguments in registers (default).

subsection.p: It is recommended to use the full APCS specifier in makefiles so the same specifier can be supplied to all tools (cc, c++, objasm, cmunge).

command-definition.name: CMunge
command-definition.description: Generates RISC OS module headers and veneers

definition-table.definition: Name the generated C header file.
Name the exported Assembler SWI header.
Name the exported C SWI header.
Add directory to include search path.
Name a dependency file for AMU.
Generate 64-bit (AArch64) code. Requires the GCC toolchain.
Generate a blank CMunge template file. When combined with -cmhg, generates a warning and produces a template containing only CMHG-compatible features.
Issue warnings for values or directives that are not compatible with CMHG.
Define Image__RO_Base in the generated header.
Generate error and veneer definitions only.

use.p: CMunge is used to automate the generation of module headers and veneers, allowing C/C++ developers to focus on the module logic rather than the low-level RISC OS interface requirements.

subsection.title: Makefile Usage
subsection.p: The following examples show how to invoke CMunge from an AMU Makefile. Two static dependency lines are required: one to declare the C header dependent on the object file, and one to build the object file from the CMHG description.

p.extended-example: h.modheader: o.modheader o.modheader: cmhg.modheader CMunge -throwback -o o.modheader -d h.modheader cmhg.modheader
h.modheader: o32.modheader o32.modheader: cmhg.modheader CMunge -32bit -throwback -o o32.modheader -d h.modheader cmhg.modheader
.SUFFIXES: .c .o .o32 .cmhg .cmhg.o .cmhg.h:; CMunge -throwback -o o.$* -d h.$* $< .cmhg.o32 .cmhg.h:; CMunge -32bit -throwback -o o32.$* -d h.$* $<

disclaimer.p: Documentation based on CMunge manual and source code.

revision.author: GCLI
revision.title: Comprehensive documentation from Manual
revision.change: Added details on toolchain internals, differences from CMHG, and expanded CLI options from the manual.

revision.author: Gerph
revision.title: Architecture, APCS and toolchain details
revision.change: Expanded architecture section to cover 26-bit, 32-bit and 64-bit support. Added APCS flag reference. Added GCC environment variables for assembler paths. Added missing CMunge-only features to differences list. Expanded LCC limitations. Documented -blank/-cmhg interaction. Added 26-bit and rule-based Makefile examples.
